clean_story: Light’s smallest unit.  Energy packets in motion. "Photon" comes from Greek phōs, meaning "light"—because each is a tiny, indivisible burst of it. We see photons as sunlight or laser beams, but they’re also silent messengers. A single photon carries energy like a tossed pebble carries momentum—except it moves at light speed and never slows down. Here’s how it works:When an electron in an atom drops to a lower energy level, it sheds the excess as a photon—like pocket change from a coin exchange. That photon zips away until it hits something: a leaf (absorbed as heat), a mirror (bounced like a ball), or your eye (triggering vision). Example:The red glow of a stop sign isn’t paint—it’s photons emitted when electrons in the sign’s atoms relax after being excited by sunlight. At night, those same atoms absorb car headlights and re-emit photons as that familiar red. Photons have no mass, yet they push solar sails in space. They stream from your Wi-Fi router as radio waves (low-energy photons) and from hospital X-ray machines as high-energy bullets. Even "empty" space hums with ancient photons from the Big Bang—now stretched into microwave static. Three related subjects are wave-particle duality, quantum entanglement and laser precision.
